game_name: Super Mario Bros.: The Lost Levels

element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Becomes deceptive; later warps send player backward through worlds
Requires visual discrimination; becomes frequent in later worlds

element_introduction_order.first_appears: After 8 completions (World 9), or unlock Worlds A-D after 8 World 8-4 clears
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Designed for expert players; gate keeps progression

typical_relief_triggers: Boss defeat (Toad declares princess in another castle)
Warp zone discovery (skip ahead, though risk of counterproductive warps)
Checkpoint vines or safe platforms after hazardous sections
typical_tension_triggers: Introduction of poison mushrooms (visual confusion with standard mushrooms)
Invisible block sequences requiring precision jumps
Wind gust sections altering mid-air trajectory
Counterproductive warps returning player to earlier worlds
Spike pits and enemy gauntlets with limited safe ground
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Escalating difficulty curve; Worlds 1-4 teach fundamentals, Worlds 5-8 demand mastery, Worlds 9-D require near-perfect execution. Tension builds gradually across each world, with brief relief at boss transitions.

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: Vertical vines indicating upward exploration; secret mushroom blocks hinted by suspicious gaps or platform arrangements
spatial_grammar.secret_convention: Invisible blocks revealed through trial or memory; warp zones embedded in level flow as optional risk/reward skips

difficulty_curve.early_game: Worlds 1-2: Refinement of SMB1 mechanics; introduces mushroom, fire flower, stars, basic warp zones. Difficulty comparable to or slightly higher than original SMB.
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Worlds 3-6: Introduction of poison mushrooms, invisible blocks, and deceptive warps. Demands split-second precision and level memorization. Significant spike in challenge.
difficulty_curve.late_game: Worlds 7-8: Relentless hazard density, mandatory invisibility block sequences, wind gusts, and boss pattern mastery. Designed for players who have internalized game mechanics.
notable_spikes: Poison mushroom first appearance (visual deception breaks trust in power-up collection)
World 3-1 and onward (invisible block sequences become mandatory rather than optional)
Worlds 5-8 (density and complexity of hazards compounds exponentially)
World 9 unlock (requires flawless warp-zone avoidance; extreme precision demands)
Worlds A-D (bonus worlds after 8 total game completions; described as 'nails-from-diamonds hard')

notable_level_patterns.description: Warp zones that appear helpful but redirect player backward or to unintended worlds, teaching distrust of shortcuts and reward mechanics.
Sequences requiring jumps on unseen platforms; enforces pixel-perfect timing and level memorization. Becomes mandatory (not optional secrets) in mid-to-late game.
Poison mushrooms placed where standard mushrooms would normally be, forcing player to avoid all power-ups or accept risk. Tests learned behavior against new threat.
Mid-air gusts alter trajectory; requires aerial control mastery and timing prediction. Combines with platforms and hazards below.
Multi-pattern bosses requiring adaptation; transition punctuated by Toad message ('Princess is in another castle'), which dampens relief through meta-narrative deception.

sources_used: tcrf: Super Mario Bros. 2 (Famicom Disk System) - The Cutting Room Floor
wikipedia: Super Mario Bros.: The Lost Levels
